title: 1066 and claims to the throne
description: The Norman Conquest: how do we know about the impact of the Conquest on England?

content.text: I can describe the succession crisis of 1066 and explain how William of Poitiers' writing can be used by historians to investigate claims to the throne.
succession crisis
a period when there is no clear heir to a position
heir
a person who expects to inherit the property or position of another person when that person dies
papal
something related to the Pope

Harold Godwinson claimed that he had been promised the throne when Edward was young.
Harold Godwinson claimed the throne because he had the support of the English earls and churchmen.
Harold Godwinson was crowned king.
Edward promised the throne to two people.
William received
papal
support for an invasion of England.
Edward the Confessor realised he was unlikely to have any children.
Edward the Confessor died.
Sort
the following events into chronological order
by numbering
them 1 to
5 with 1 as the earliest.
Historians rely upon sources to help them complete their work.
Sources include written documents, images and artefacts (objects) produced in the past.
These sources help to inform historians about the periods they are studying.
When historians use sources, they have to think carefully about them.

Historians not only need to consider what sources may suggest about the periods they are studying. It is also necessary to consider why sources may have been produced and how reliable (trustworthy) the information they provide seems to be.

content.text: Illustration of William of Normandy based on the Bayeux Tapestry
Poitiers mentions the bloodline connection
established by Edward’s mother, Emma of Normandy, in order to add legitimacy to William’s claim.
Poitiers claims that Edward chose William as his
heir
early in his reign.
Extract A
While Edward was considering the favours bestowed upon him by William, as well as the
close bloodline connection
which existed between them, [...] he decided to
establish William as the rightful
heir
to the [English] crown.
Poitiers claims that Harold promised to be loyal to William, so
that he can present his later actions as treacherous, strengthening William’s claim.
Poitiers asserts that Harold promised to support William’s claim to the throne. This suggests that Harold broke a holy (sacred) promise and depicts him as dishonest, strengthening William’s claim.
Extract B
Harold
pledged fealty
to William in a sacred rite. [...] He swore ‘that he would ensure
the English monarchy would be confirmed in William’s hands
after Edward’s death.’
Both Edward and Harold had promised the English throne to William.
Only Edward had promised the English throne to William.
Only Harold had promised the English throne to William.
What does the Gesta Guillelmi claim?
Historians can say with certainty that Edward promised William the throne.
Historians have to treat claims in Norman sources, like Poitiers’ Gesta Guillelmi, carefully as they were often made to flatter William.
He wanted to portray Harold as an honourable man.
He wanted to damage Harold’s claim to the throne.
He wanted to discredit William’s claim to the throne.
Why did William of Poitiers describe Harold’s oath to William?
List
three
ways in which the Gesta Guillelmi seeks to justify or strengthen William’s claim to the throne:
List
three
ways in which the Gesta Guillelmi seeks to justify or strengthen William’s claim to the throne:
Your answers could include:
It states that Edward the Confessor promised William the throne.
It states that Harold promised to support William’s claim.
It states that Harold was a treacherous liar who broke his promises.
Answer
the following question in
two
or
three
sentences:
When using the Gesta Guillelmi, why do historians
need to keep in mind that Poitiers wrote it in order
to promote William’s claim to the throne?
You could consider the following in your answer:
Poitiers’ role
Poitiers’ motive
When using the Gesta Guillelmi, why do historians
need to keep in mind that Poitiers wrote it in order to promote William’s claim to the throne? Your answer could include:
Historians need to keep in mind that Poitiers was writing in order to promote William’s claim because this would have influenced his work. Poitiers was
a member of William’s household and served as William’s priest
and therefore
he had a motive to flatter and praise William
, rather than remaining ‘bound by the truth’. Subsequently, his claims that William had been promised the throne and that Harold was a liar need to be approached with caution by historians.
When Edward the Confessor died, there was no
heir
to the throne.
Both William of Normandy and Harold Godwinson claimed the throne.
William of Poitiers’ Gesta Guillelmi is an important source which supported William’s claim to the throne by claiming that Harold had promised to support William and uphold the Norman’s own claim.
The Gesta Guillelmi has to be read with caution by historians because William of Poitiers may have been motivated to report some events inaccurately.
